coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor for selective and sensitive detection of copper (II) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zhifeng
Deng, Peihong
Li, Junhua
Tang, Siping
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, a fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor (FIIS) for rapid and convenient detection of Cu2+ ions was fabricated. A fluorescent polymerizable ligand, i.e., 4-(2-aminomethyl)pyridine-N-allylnaphthalimide, was designed and synthesized. The FIIS was prepared by surface functionalization of PVDF membrane with a thin layer of copper (II) ion-imprinted polymer using the synthesized ligand as the fluorescent functional monomer. The intensity of fluorescence emission of FIIS decreased linearly with the increase of copper (II) ions concentration in the range of 0–70.0μM. The results of selectivity tests indicated that FIIS has high specific recognition ability for Cu2+ ions. The recoveries for the spiked samples were in the range of 96.4–104.4%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were found to be 2.17–4.75%. The FIIS was successfully applied to the determination of copper (II) ions in real water samples. The Limits of detection (LODs) for Cu2+ ions in real water samples were in the range of 0.11–0.14 uM. The present study provided a feasible strategy for construction of fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor for convenient, sensitive and selective detection of metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper (II) ion
Ion imprinted polymer
1,8-naphthalimide
Ion recognition
Fluorescence emission

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabricating fluorescent cu2+ ions imprinted sensor (FIIS)
Binding kinetics tests of FIIS for Cu (II) ions
Fluorescence responses of FIIS and FNIS
Selectivity and interference investigation of FIIS and FNIS
Spiking experiments and analysis of real water samples
Synthesis and spectroscopic property of the fluorescent monomers (F)
The effect of incubation time and pH on the fluorescence intensity of FIIS
Selectivity and sensitivity studies of FIIS for Cu 2+ ions
Application for the detection of Cu2+ ions in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Exitation and emission spectra of F (20 uM) in ethanol.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of F (30 uM) in ethanol-water mixture (1:4, v/v) containing different concentrations of Cu2+ ions. From top to bottom: 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80μM Cu2+ ions respectively. (B) Fluorescence responses of F (30 uM) to various metal ions (50 uM) in ethanol-water mixture (1:4, v/v).
(a) PVDF membrane (1), FIIS (2) and FNIS (3) under visible light (I) and UV irradiation (II). (b) SEM images of PVDF membrane (1), FIIS (2) and FNIS (3).
IR spectra of PVDF membrane, FIIS and FNIS.
Excitation (1, 3), emission (2, 4) scans of FIIS (1, 2) and FNIS (3, 4) and the emission scan of original PVDF membrane (5). The excitation and emission slit widths were 3nm.

(a) Fluorescence response time of FIIS for Cu2+ ions (fluorescence intensities were measure at 493nm, λex =438nm, concentration of Cu2+ ions: 50uM); (b) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of FIIS in 50mM PBS buffer. The pH was adjusted by adding concentrated hydrochloric acid or NaOH solution (8.0M).
Fluorescence emission intensities of FIIS (a) and FNIS (b) after incubation with different metal ions and anions (50 uM) in aqueous solutions.
Images of FIIS and FNIS under visible light and UV light after incubation with different transition metal ions.
SEM images of FIIS (1, 2) and FNIS (3, 4) before (1, 3) and after (2, 4) the treatment of Cu2+ ions.

Fluorescent spectra of FIIS (a) and FNIS (b) after incubation with different concentrations of Cu (II) ions. From top to bottom: 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70μM Cu2+, respectively. Inset: the corresponding Stern–Volmer plots.

Relative fluorescence response intensity of recycled FIIS to Cu2+ ions. ΔI i/ΔI 1 =(I i(afterbinding)-I i(beforebinding))/(I 1(afterbinding)-I 1(beforebinding)), wherein, “i” means the recycle times, and “1” represents the 1st use; Fuorescence intensities were measure at 493nm, λex =438nm; Concentration of Cu2+ ions: 50 uM.
Accuracy and precision of the method.
Limits of detection (LODs), limits of quantification (LOQs) and Cu2+ ions content in real water samples.
Comparison of different methods for Cu2+ ions detection.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor for selective and sensitive detection of copper (II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hengyang Normal University, Hengyang, 421008, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Organometallic Materials of Hunan Province University, Hengyang, 421008, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Organometallic Materials of Hunan Province University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hengyang Normal University, Hengyang, 421008, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, a fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor (FIIS) for rapid and convenient detection of Cu2+ ions was fabricated. A fluorescent polymerizable ligand, i.e., 4-(2-aminomethyl)pyridine-N-allylnaphthalimide, was designed and synthesized. The FIIS was prepared by surface functionalization of PVDF membrane with a thin layer of copper (II) ion-imprinted polymer using the synthesized ligand as the fluorescent functional monomer. The intensity of fluorescence emission of FIIS decreased linearly with the increase of copper (II) ions concentration in the range of 0–70.0μM. The results of selectivity tests indicated that FIIS has high specific recognition ability for Cu2+ ions. The recoveries for the spiked samples were in the range of 96.4–104.4%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were found to be 2.17–4.75%. The FIIS was successfully applied to the determination of copper (II) ions in real water samples. The Limits of detection (LODs) for Cu2+ ions in real water samples were in the range of 0.11–0.14 uM. The present study provided a feasible strategy for construction of fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor for convenient, sensitive and selective detection of metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is an indispensable trace element for life and plays an important role in various biological processes. It serves as a catalytic cofactor for a variety of metalloenzymes, including superoxide dismutase, cytochrome coxidase, and tyrosinase [1,2]. But excess copper (II) ions may be highly toxic to organisms, resulting in gastrointestinal distress, liver or kidney damage, and serious neurodegenerative diseases [3,4]. copper (II) ion is also a significant metal pollutant due to its extensive applications. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set the maximum allowable level of copper in drinking water at 1.3ppm (20μM). Therefore, it is of considerable significance to develop advanced methods for effective detection of Cu2+ ions level in water. The standard procedures for Cu2+ ions detection are instrument-based analytical methods, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICPMS), graphite furnace atomic absorption spectroscopy (GFAAS), and atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy (AAS/AES), etc. In recent years, various methods, including fluorimetric detection [5–12], chemiluminescence immunoassay [13], colorimetric assay [14], surface-enhanced raman scattering [15], electrochemical measurement [16], DNA enzymes [17] and so on [18–21] have been developed for the detection of Cu2+ ions. These methods have distinct advantages in certain situations, but each of them has its own disadvantages, such as requiring expensive instrument, complicated sample preparations, tedious operation procedures, and poor selectivity. Pursuing for a convenient, sensitive, selective and inexpensive method for detection of Cu2+ ions is very essential for both environment and human health.

Molecular imprinting is a technique to create template-shaped cavities in polymer matrices with memory of the template molecules to be used in molecular recognition. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been widely used in many fields [22–27] due to their excellent properties such as high selectivity, chemical and mechanical stabilities, low cost and ease of fabrications. Fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymers (FMIPs), which have combined the specific recognition ability of MIPs and the high sensitivity property of fluorescent detection, have gained increasing attentions in recent years [28–32].

Ion-imprinted polymers (IIPs), which were prepared using polymerizable ligands as functional monomers and metal ions as ion templates, have recognition ability for a specific ion. The application of IIPs in metal ions analysis and detection has received a significant amount of attention in recent years [33–45]. Accordingly, some imprinting strategies have been developed for preparation of Cu (II) ion-imprinted polymers [39–44].

However, fabricating fluorescent ion-imprinted polymers (FIIPs) is still a challenging work, since most metal ions have no fluorescent characteristics. For fabricating FIIPs, fluorescent polymerizable ligands, which is served as the functional monomers, need to be specially designed and synthesized. The fluorescent polymerizable ligand must have at least two functions: to form interactions with (chelate with) the target metal ion and to generate detectable fluorescence responses when the interaction takes place. So far, seldom reports have been found on fabricating FIIPs based on fluorescent functional monomer [40,46]. For preparation of silver ion-imprinted fluorescent on-off chemosensor, Sun et al. synthesized a fluorescent functional monomer based on a fluorescein derivative [46]. In another study, a novel functional monomer, 4-[(E)-2-(4′-methyl-2,2′-bipyridin-4-yl)vinyl] phenyl methacrylate was designed and synthesized by Pinheiro’s group for preparation of fluorescent copper ion-imprinted polymer [40].

1,8-naphthalimide derivatives are the important building blocks for construction of various fluorogenic materials owing to their excellent properties such as high fluorescence quantum yield, good stability, large Stokes shift and being easily modified, etc [47,48]. 1,8-naphthalimide derivatives have also been used as fluorescent functional monomer for fabricating FMIPs [49–51]. For example, Rouhani et al. [49] reported the synthesis of FMIP for recognition of caffeine using N-allyl-4-ethylenediamine-1,8-naphthalimide as the fluorescent functional monomer. To the best of our knowledge, the study on application of 1,8-naphthalimide derivatives in fabricating fluorescent ion-imprinted polymers has not been reported.

The aim of this work was to fabricate a fluorescent sensor based on ion-imprinting technique for convenient, sensitive and selective detection of copper (II) ions. In the present study, the utilization of 1,8-naphthalimide derivative as fluorescent functional monomer in ion imprinting was investigated. Taking the commercially available 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride as the starting material, a fluorescent polymerizable ligand, i.e., 4-(2-aminomethyl)pyridine-N- allylnaphthalimide, was first synthesized. Then, a fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor (FIIS) for detection of copper (II) ions was fabricated by anchoring ion-imprinted polymer layer on the surface of PVDF through the surface imprinting technique using the synthesized 1,8-naphthalimide derivative as the functional monomer. The intensity of fluorescence emission of FIIS was found to decrease when increasing amount of copper (II) ions was added. The prepared FIIS exhibited high selective and sensitive recognition ability for copper (II) ions and was successfully applied to the determination of copper (II) ions in real water samples.

2-(aminomethyl) pyridine was purchased from J&K Scientific LTD. (Beijing, China). 4-Bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride (98%) was obtained from Adamas (Shanghai, China). 2-Methoxymethanol (99%) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Allyamine and 2-vinylpyridine were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) was obtained from Jiangsu Anli Chemical Plant (Jiangsu, China). Before use, EGDMA was distilled under vacuum after being extracted with 10% sodium hydroxide and dried over anhydrous magnesium sulfate. 2, 2-azobis(isobutyronitrile) (AIBN) was obtained from Shanghai Chemical Plant (Shanghai, China) and recrystallized from methanol. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) film (0.45 um) was purchased from Tansoole (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was dried with molecular sieves 3A and then distilled under reduced pressure. Deionized water (18.2MΩcm) obtained from a Hitech laboratory water purification system (Hitech Instruments Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) was used throughout the experiments. All other chemicals used were of analytical grade.

The FT-IR spectra were recorded on an IRPrestige21 instrument (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence emission was measured using a RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The elemental analysis was measured on a Vario EL CHNS Elemental Analyzer (Elementar, Germany). The 1H NMR spectra were measured on a Bruker AV 500MHz spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging was carried out on an EVO MA10 (Zeiss, Germany) scanning electron microscope.
The fluorescent polymerizable ligand (F) was synthesized via two steps from 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride. Scheme 1 describes the processes of synthesis of the fluorescent monomer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabricating fluorescent cu2+ ions imprinted sensor (FIIS)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor (FIIS) was prepared as following: 0.0686g (0.2mmol) of fluorescent functional monomer (F) and 0.0105g (0.1mmol) of 2-vinylpyridine as co-functional monomer were dissolved in 7.0ml of DMSO. Then 0.5ml of 0.1M CuCl2 (0.05mmol) solution was added to the above mixture. After being stirred for 30min, 2.0mmol of EGDMA as the cross-linker and 0.020g of AIBN as the initiator were added to the mixture. The polymerizable mixture was deoxygenated by bubbling with nitrogen for 10min. A PVDF film (pore size 0.45μm) was soaked in 0.15M AIBN solution (in acetonitrile) for 10min, and then soaked in the prepared polymerizable mixture for 30min. Then the PVDF film was clamped between two glass plates, sealed with scotch tape and then polymerized for 12h under a UV lamp (18W, 365nm). For removal of Cu (II) ions and the unreacted reagents, the prepared FIIS was washed with 0.1M EDTA solution, the mixture of methanol and water (50:50, V/V) and methanol successively until the fluorescent functional monomer in the elution could no longer be detected by fluorescence emission measurement. The fluorescent non-imprinted sensor (FNIS) was synthesized with the same procedure as the FIIS without adding CuCl2 during the polymerization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding kinetics tests of FIIS for Cu (II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to probe the binding kinetic of Cu (II) ions on FIIS and to assess the accessibility, the response time of the fabricated FIIS was investigated. FIIS was suspended in CuCl2 solution (50μM) in calibrated test tube. The sensor was taken out after 2min and dried by purging with nitrogen gas. Then FIIS was fixed on a sample holder and its fluorescence emission spectrum was scanned. The fluorescence emission intensity was measured at 493nm (λex =438nm). The excitation and emission silt widths were set at 3nm for FIIS. After fluorescent scanning, the FIIS was immersed in the above CuCl2 solution again and then taken out after another 2min. After drying, the fluorescence intensity was measured under the conditions as described above. The immersion-drying-scanning cycle was repeated until the fluorescence intensity reached to a constant value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of FIIS and FNIS
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of investigating the fluorescence responses of FIIS and FNIS to Cu (II) ions and evaluate the possibility of quantitative detection of Cu (II) ions with FIIS, FIIS and FNIS were suspended in CuCl2 solutions with concentrations as 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 and 70μM in calibrated test tubes respectively for 14min, and then dried by purging with nitrogen gas. Then the fluorescence emission intensities of FIIS and FNIS were measured as described in Section 2.4. The fluorescence emission intensity was measured at 493nm (λex =438nm) for FIIS and 484nm for FNIS (λex =425nm). The excitation and emission slit widths were set at 3nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference investigation of FIIS and FNIS
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the prepared FIIS, and investigate about the possibility to detect Cu2+ ions in complicated samples, FIIS was suspended in 50μM of Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Al3+, F−, OAc−, CN−, SH−, S2− solutions in calibrated test tubes respectively for 14min for adsorption of ions, and then dried by purging with nitrogen gas. The fluorescence spectra of FIIS before and after adsorption of each ion were recorded. The selectivity test of FNIS was also performed using the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spiking experiments and analysis of real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Double-distilled water was used for the spiking experiments. Water samples spiked with Cu2+ ions at concentrations of 5.0, 10.0, 20.0 and 30.0μM were prepared and detected with FIIS directly under the conditions as described in Section 2.4.

All real water samples were collected in pre-rinsed brown glass bottles and were stored at 4°C and analyzed within 24h of collection. The tap water sample was collected from our lab. The drinking water sample (C'estbon pure water produced at Shenzhen China) was obtained from a supermarket. The river water sample was collected from Hengyang section of the Xiangjiang River. The industrial effluents wastewater sample was collected from a factory of Hunan Shuikoushan Nonferrous Metals Group Co., Ltd. (Hengyang, China). The water samples were filtered through a 0.45μm filter to remove sediments or particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and spectroscopic property of the fluorescent monomers (F)
ce:section.ce:para: The spectroscopic properties of 1,8-naphthalimides are strongly dependent on the substituent at C-4 of the naphthalene ring. For increasing fluorescent quantum yield, the substituent group at the 4-position of 1,8-naphthalimide should be electron-donating group. Another requirement of the substituent group at C-4 is that it can chelate with the target metal ion. Taking these considerations into account, 2-aminomethyl pyridine was chosen as the C-4 substituent group in the present study for synthesis of the fluorescent functional monomer (F). F has been conveniently synthesized from 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride via amidation and amination and its structure was characterized by IR, 1H NMR and EA. As we expected, F displayed fluorescent property. Fig. 1 shows that when F in ethanol was excited at 444nm, it emitted fluorescence with maximum emission at 529nm. In order to probe the fluorescence sensing of chromophore against Cu2+ ions, a titration of monomer with Cu2+ ions was performed. Fig. 2 A shows the changes of fluorescence intensity of F in the presence of various concentrations of Cu2+ ions. Clearly, the fluorescence intensity of F exhibited a gradual decrease upon the addition of Cu2+ ions. When Cu2+ ions concentration increased from 0 to 80μM, the fluorescent intensity of F decreased from 596 to 193 (a.u.). The fluorescence quenching of F moiety by Cu2+ ions could be ascribed to a photo-induced energy transfer mechanism and/or a d–d electron paramagnetic quenching mechanism [52,53]. The association constant are obtained as K =3.98×106 Lmol−1 according to the equation: lg(I0 /I-1)=lgK +nlg[Cu2+], where I0 and I are the fluorescent intensity of F in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions, respectively, K is the association constant and n is the number of binding sites [8] (Supporting Information, Fig. S1).

To investigate the selectivity of F for metal ions, various metal ions were mixed with it in ethanol-water mixture (1:4, v/v). Fig. 2B illustrates the fluorescence responses of F to various metal ions. We can see that besides Cu2+ ions, other selected ions including Hg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Ca2+ and Al3+ ions can also quench the fluorescence emission of F in various degree. The degrees of fluorescence quenching caused by Hg2+, Pb2+, Co2+ and Mn2+ ions are similar to that of Cu2+ ions. The results indicate that the fluorescent functional monomer doesn't have obvious selectivity for Cu2+ ions.

Surface imprinting strategy is widely used in molecular imprinting technique because it can control the imprint sites to be situated at the surface or in the proximity of the material surface, which can improve the accessibility and decrease the response time. In the present study, surface imprinting strategy was adopted and PVDF membrane was used as the supporter for preparation of FIIS. An additional advantage of choosing PVDF membrane as the supporter is that it can improve the flexibility and mechanical stability of the sensor. The fluorescent ion-imprinted sensing material was fabricated on the surface of PVDF membrane by photoinitiated grafting polymerization method. When using fluorescent monomers for preparation of MIPs, the obtained MIPs often show high background fluorescence because of the presence of randomly positioned fluorescent functional monomer residues in the polymer matrixes [54]. To reduce the fluorescence intensity of the background, a non-fluorescent monomer, i.e. 2-vinylpyridine, was chosen as a co-functional monomer for imprinting. Accordingly, the dosage of the fluorescent monomer (F) was reduced.

Appearances of FIIS, FNIS and PVDF membrane are shown in Fig. 3 a. Under UV irradiation, FIIS and FNIS showed remarkable brightness due to the presence of fluorophores in polymer backbone. Scanning electron microscopy images of FIIS, FNIS and PVDF membrane are shown in Fig. 3b. The original PVDF membrane shows regular porous structure obviously. After the polymerization reaction, a layer of polymer has been formed on the surface of PVDF membrane and most of the big pores in PVDF membrane have been covered. The changes of the membrane morphology indicated that the ion-imprinted or non-imprinted polymer film can be produced on the surface of the PVDF membrane by the radical polymerization reaction.

Then the PVDF membrane, the prepared FIIS and FNIS were characterized by FTIR, respectively (Fig. 4 ). The IR spectrum of PVDF shows the characteristic CF2 group absorption bands at 1244 and 1097cm−1. After the polymerization reaction, the characteristic absorption bands of CO (1730cm−1), CH stretching vibration (2854, 2920cm−1) and OH stretching vibration (3455cm−1) can be observed obviously on the IR spectra of FIIS and FNIS. The results of FTIR indicated that the ion-imprinted or non-imprinted polymer has been successfully grafted onto PVDF membrane.

The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of FIIS and FNIS were measured. As a control, the original PVDF membrane was also measured under the same conditions. As can be seen in Fig. 5 , FIIS displayed a maximum fluorescence emission at 493nm when excited at 438nm, while FNIS had a maximal excitation of 382nm and a maximal emission of 484nm. The original PVDF membrane displayed almost no fluorescence emission. It has been observed that the separation between the absorption and the emission bands of the fluorescent monomer (F) was smaller in a rigid polymer matrix than in the liquid monomer. This phenomenon was also observed in another report [49]. It might be ascribed to that the surroundings of the fluorescent monomer, which play an important role on stabilization of ground and transition state of dye molecule, are different from each other. The fluorescence measurement results can further verify the successful formation of ion-imprinted polymer film on the surface of the PVDF membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of incubation time and pH on the fluorescence intensity of FIIS
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the accessibility of FIIS for Cu2+ ions, we investigated the effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of FIIS. After different incubation times, the fluorescence intensities of FIIS at 493nm have been measured (Fig. 6 a). The figure shows that the emission intensity at the initial stage of incubation decreased fast with increasing contact time and gradually slowed down until reaching a stable value at about 14min. The constant value signified an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Therefore, 14min was chosen as the incubation time for the following experiments. The time needed to reach equilibrium binding is quite short, which can be attributed to the thin imprinting layer formed on the surface of PVDF membrane. The prepared sensor demonstrated a short response time for the target ion, which is very important for its practical application in metal ions sensing.

Fluorophores are usually disturbed by coexist protons when they are used for practical metal ions analysis and detection. The effect of pH on the fluorescence of FIIS was investigated. As can be seen in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence emission intensities of FIIS are strongly influenced by pH value in the PBS buffer solutions. Enhanced fluorescence emission intensity was obtained in the pH 6–8 range. In view of the fact that Cu2+ would form insoluble precipitate of Cu(OH)2 in basic aqueous solutions, the suitable pH value for Cu2+ ions determination is in the range of 6–7. It suggested that no buffer solution is required for the detection of Cu2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity studies of FIIS for Cu 2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of FIIS and FNIS for Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence emission intensities of FIIS and FNIS were measured in the presence of several different metal ions under identical conditions. Fig. 7 shows the emission intensities of FIIS and FNIS on adding Hg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Ca2+ and Al3+ ions. Fig. S2 (see Supporting Information) shows the emission fluorescence spectra of FIIS with different metal ions. For FIIS (Fig. 7a), we can see that the largest decrease in emission intensity was caused by Cu2+ ions. No significant change in the fluorescence intensity was observed for the other tested metal ions, indicating that the other metal ions do not interfere significantly with the binding of FIIS to Cu2+ ions. The results of selective tests indicate that FIIS has high selectivity for Cu2+ ions and the interference of other metal ions can be removed by ion imprinting technique. This is because that many specific recognition sites were formed for Cu2+ ions in the imprinted polymer layer of FIIS by ion imprinting. Fig. 7b shows that no obvious fluorescence changes can be observed for FNIS after immersing in any of the metal ions solution. In the polymer layer of FNIS, there are no specific recognition sites for any of the metal ions.

Additionally, some common anion ions, such as F−, OAc−, CN−, SH−, S2−,were also tested as the potential interferents to evaluate the specificity of the prepared sensor. It can be seen from Fig. 7 that there is almost no effect of these anions on the emission intensities of FIIS and FNIS. This could be easily explained because the fluorescent functional monomer of FIIS and FNIS can not chelate with these anions, thus the fluorescence emission of the functional monomer has no obvious change.

Images of FIIS and FNIS under visible light and UV irradiation after incubation with different transition metal ions, such as Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, and Mn2+ ions, were shown in Fig. 8 . It can be seen that for FIIS, the brightness decreased obviously after incubation with Cu2+ ions under UV irradiation, while the other tested metal ions could not cause obvious brightness variation. The results confirmed that FIIS has high selectivity for Cu2+ ions. Fig. 8 shows that under visible light, the brightnesses of FIIS and FNIS have no obvious change after incubation with different transition metal ions.

SEM images of FIIS and FNIS before and after the treatment of Cu2+ ions were shown in Fig. 9 . It was found that after incubation with Cu2+ ions, no obvious morphological change was observed in both FIIS and FNIS.

The sensitivity of FIIS for Cu2+ ions detection was also investigated. As presented in Fig. 10 a, the fluorescence intensity of FIIS can be quenched by Cu2+ ions sensitively. When the Cu2+ concentration increased from 0 to 70.0μM, the fluorescence intensity of FIIS decreased from 997 to 274 (a.u.) (measured at 493nm). For FNIS, the fluorescence emission intensity had no significant change with the increase of Cu2+ ions concentration. The fluorescence intensity of FNIS decreased from 698 to 632 (a.u.) (measured at 484nm) when Cu2+ ions concentration increased from 0 to 70.0μM (Fig. 10b).

When the imprinted material is exposed to various concentrations of Cu2+ ions (as a quencher), the dependence of the fluorescence signal in response to Cu2+ ions can be described by the Stern–Volmer equation:
4 I0 /I =1+ KSV [Q]
Where I0 and I are the emission intensity of FIIS in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ ions. [Q] is the concentration of the quencher and KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant. When (I0 /I −1) is plotted against [Q], a linear relationship was obtained in the range of 0–70.0μM Cu2+ ions concentrations, with a correlation coefficient of R2 =0.972. The slope of the curve is equal to KSV . The KSV of FIIS was calculated to be 18.99mM−1. On the basis of the quenching amount of the fluorescence intensity of FIIS with Cu2+ ions, FIIS can be used as a fluorescent sensor to detect template Cu2+ ions.

The developed method was validated in terms of accuracy and precision (%RSD). Accuracy of the method was determined by calculating the recoveries in the measurement of samples spiked at different concentration levels (5.0, 10.0, 20.0 and 30.0μM) based on three replicate determinations. Precision was calculated as the relative standard deviation of the determined concentration. The recoveries and the RSDs of the method were calculated and summarized in Table 1 . From Table 1, it can be seen that the recoveries were in the range of 96.4–104.4% while the RSDs were found to be 2.17–4.75%, which indicated that the accuracy of the proposed method were satisfactory.

To ensure stability, the prepared FIIS was kept in the dark when not in use. The stability of the FIIS was investigated through repeated detection of the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent sensor every week. The intensity of FIIS retained 95.5% of its initial intensity after storage for 1 month, which implied that the fluorescence sensor had excellent storage stability.

The reusability of the FIIS was also investigated. The regeneration experiments were repeated using the same FIIS. The fluorescence intensities of FIIS before and after binding of Cu2+ ions during each reuse cycle were recorded. And the relative fluorescence emission intensity at wavelength of 493nm has been calculated for each reuse cycle with the formula of ΔI i/ΔI 1 =(I i(afterbinding)-I i(beforebinding))/(I 1(afterbinding)-I 1(beforebinding)). Herein, “i” means the recycle time, and “1” means the 1st use [46]. The results are shown in Fig. 11 . It can be seen that after 10 cycles of the removing/rebinding process, the relative fluorescence response intensity of the FIIS was maintained at almost 90% of the original value, which indicated that the reusability of the prepared FIIS was satisfactory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for the detection of Cu2+ ions in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: After simple filtration, four real water samples (i.e., the tap water sample, the C'estbon pure water sample, the river water sample and the industrial wastewater sample) were analysised by the method developed in this work and ICP-MS. The results are given in Table 2 . As can be seen from Table 2, the C'estbon pure water sample did not contain any detectable amount of Cu2+ ions. However, Cu2+ ions was detected in the other three water samples. The Cu2+ ions concentrations found in the tap water sample, the Xiangjiang river water sample and the industrial wastewater sample were 0.13, 0.23 and 2.26 uM, respectively. The results obtained based on FIIS were in agreement with those obtained by the ICP-MS measurement, indicating that the prepared FIIS can be applied to determination of Cu2+ ions content in real water samples.

For determination of the limits of detection (LODs) and limits of quantification (LOQs), the four real water samples were spiked with Cu2+ ions at the levels of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0 uM and were analyzed according to the method described in Section 2.4. The LODs and LOQs, defined as the amounts of the analytes at a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3 and 10, respectively, were calculated and are listed in Table 2. It can be seen that the LODs and LOQs for Cu2+ ions in real water samples were in the range of 0.11–0.14 uM and 0.37–0.47 uM, respectively. The United States defined maximum contaminant level for Cu2+ ions in tap water is 20 uM, therefore, the prepared FIIS is suitable for Cu2+ ions detection under practical conditions.

The performance of the present FIIS sensor was compared with other methods for Cu2+ ions determination in literatures (Table 3 ). Although the LODs of present work are higher than those of some methods, such as fluorimetric detection [5,8,55,56], electrochemical detection [16] and immunoassay [58], it is still lower than or close to those of other works including surface-enhanced raman scattering[15], carbon dots sensor [11], visual volumetric hydrogel sensor [18], and colorimetric assay [59,60], etc. [57]. Furthermore, the method developed in this work has its unique advantages such as high selectivity, low cost, ease of operation, etc. Therefore, the present FIIS sensor is a promising candidate for Cu2+ ions determination in water environment.

In this study, the utilization of 1,8-naphthalimide derivative as fluorescent functional monomer in ion imprinting was investigated. A fluorescent functional monomer (F) was first designed and synthesized. Then a fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor (FIIS) for detection of copper (II) ions was fabricated by combination the advantages of ion-imprinting technique and fluorescent detection technique. The prepared FIIS displayed a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent response toward copper (II) ions. The FIIS was successfully applied to the determination of copper (II) ions in real water samples. The study proved that 1,8-naphthalimide can be used as a modular building block for construction of fluorogenic ion-imprinted materials. The study provided a feasible strategy for construction of fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor for convenient, sensitive and selective detection of metal ions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21472038 and 21505035), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (No. 2017JJ2007, 2016JJ3028), the Open Fund of Key Laboratory of Functional Organometallic Materials of Ordinary University in Hunan Province (No. 17K013), the Aid Programs for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province for the financial support of the research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.09.007.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhifeng Xu received his M.S. degree in Chemistry from Central China Normal University, Wuhan, China in 1999. In 2006, he received his Ph.D. degree in Chemistry at Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, China. The same year he joined Hengyang Normal University as a teacher in the College of Chemistry and Materials Science. He has worked as a visiting Scientist in Sweden from Oct. 2011 to Oct. 2012 at Lund University. Currently, he is a professor at Hengyang Normal University. His main research interest is design and preparation of novel functional materials for affinity separation.

Peihong Deng received her B.S. degree in Chemistry from Guangxi University, Nanning, China in 1997. She received her M.S. degree in Chemistry from Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, China in 2000. She joined Hengyang Normal University as a teacher in the College of Chemistry and Materials Science in 2001. Currently, she is professor and engaged in teaching of chemistry at Hengyang Normal University. Her main research interest is construction and application of electrochemical sensors.

Junhua Li received his B.S. degree in Chemistry from Yangtze University, Jingzhou, China in 2003. In 2006, he received his M.S. degree in Chemistry at Central China Normal University, Wuhan, China. In 2016, he received his Ph.D. degree in Chemistry from Central South University. He joined Hengyang Normal University as a teacher in the College of Chemistry and Materials Science in 2006. His research interests cover chemical modified electrodes and chemical sensors.

Siping Tang received her M.S. degree in Chemistry from Hunan Normal University, Changsha, China in 2001. In 2010, she received her Ph.D. degree in Chemistry at Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, China. The same year she joined Hengyang Normal University as a teacher in the College of Chemistry and Materials Science. Currently, she is a professor at Hengyang Normal University. Her research interest is focused on organic analysis and separation.
